Source: MarketGrader

For now, though, GLIN's sector breakdown leans toward beneficiaries of ongoing regime shifts. Financials, the current largest allocation at 26.4%, stands to benefit from monetary and regulatory easing by the central bank (see HDFC Bank: Asia's Compounding Machine Is Back On Track). Meanwhile, third-largest Consumer Discretionary (14.7%) stands out as a beneficiary of the government’s growing shift toward boosting consumption. Last but not least is GLIN’s second-largest exposure, Information Technology (19.4%), and its leverage to a pickup in IT budgets globally (see Infosys: Quality Large-Cap Levered To An IT Spending Recovery).

As for the single-stock split, there have been a few changes since last quarter. At the top, ICICI Bank (IBN) is now the largest holding at 6.0%, displacing IT services company HCL Technologies (second-largest holding at 5.7%). The rest of the top holdings list is similarly dominated by banks (State Bank of India at 5.6%) and IT services (Infosys (INFY) at 5.1% and Tata Consultancy Services at 5.0%).

Other notable shifts include GLIN’s increased small-cap exposure (up over two percentage points to 5.2%) at the expense of mid (13.9%) and large-caps (80.5%).

GLIN Performance – Down but Not Out

Deploying a growth-oriented strategy tends to work well in bull markets but, as the last quarter has shown, not so much in bear markets. Per GLIN’s latest disclosures, the fund is down -8.6% in NAV terms year-to-date and up only +2.0% over the last year – slightly behind key peer EPI’s +3.7% one-year return. The more defensive of the Indian 'factor' ETFs, Invesco’s large-cap-focused India ETF (see PIN: Riding Out The Indian Storm), has been the key one-year outperformer at +4.1%.

Zooming out, though, GLIN has still compounded at a respectable +5.9% and +6.6% pace over the last three and five years, respectively. GLIN has, however, lagged behind both EPI and PIN’s more straightforward stock selection strategies since tracking the MarketGrader India All Cap Growth Leaders Index in 2020.

Given its underwhelming track record, why then invest in GLIN over EPI or PIN? Ultimately, it comes down to the process; specifically, buying Indian growth without overpaying for it (i.e., 'GARP').

GLIN's headline portfolio stats, compiled by MarketGrader, make this clear – investors pay a lower P/E (~25x trailing) for more growth (~35% three-year average; ~28% one-year) and returns on equity (~18%). The fund’s growth orientation also allows it to outperform in upcycles (like in 2023/H1 2024), so those looking to express a more optimistic view on the Indian cycle will find more to like here.

Fundamental Attributes

Forward P/E Ratio (12 Mo.)

25.0

Avg. EPS Growth - 1 Yr.

27.8%

Avg. EPS Growth - 3 Yr.

35.3%

Avg. Return on Equity

17.9%

Source: MarketGrader

Summing Up

India has fallen out of favor in recent months and higher ‘beta’ growth funds like GLIN have led the way lower. But the fundamental direction of travel, from inflation to policy stimulus, is positive; India’s status as the fastest-growing large economy also remains structurally intact. Ahead of a cyclical swing, GLIN’s status as an economically sensitive ‘GARP’ fund, makes it a prime rebound candidate.

data.title: Comcast: The P/E Ratio Is Crazy Low, Is It A Buy
data.description:
Comcast (NASDAQ:CMCSA) stock took a dramatic 10% plunge the day it released its Q4 and full year 2024 results. Since then, the stock has managed to claw back a little under half of the losses. Since its earnings ratio is now in the single digits, many are questioning if it's finally time to dive in.

Memo To Management: Never Overpromise

Considering that the numbers themselves really weren’t that bad, the proximate cause of the decline appears to have been the erroneous estimates management themselves had previously released. Comcast leadership had indicated that it expected broadband losses of 100k give or take just weeks prior to New Years; it wound up reporting losing 139,000 broadband subs, which meant that the last month of 2024 must have been just horrible.

It’s true that Hurricanes Milton and Helene hit in Q4, but it seems unlikely that was the cause of the overshoot. Those hurricanes were less violent than Hurricane Ian, the Florida hurricane of 2022 and one of the worst in recorded history. Comcast put broadband losses from that natural disaster at 22,000 total. Management had indicated that they expected these hurricane losses to only be around 10,000.

Author History

It was almost nine years ago, I wrote an article arguing that Comcast no longer represented a good investment, given the changes coming in the Pay-TV system.

At the time, I was concerned primarily with the rather esoteric issue of FCC regulatory initiatives pertaining to the consumer pricing of carrier-branded set-top box equipment…..and if that topic doesn’t put you to sleep, nothing will.

My choice of approach may be questionable, but my results proved solid. Since that article, Comcast has risen a grand total of 13%; the S&P 500 has nearly tripled over the same time frame. I have periodically returned to it since then, trying to keep readers updated on the many issues touching on a corporate behemoth as large as Comcast, but my conclusion has never really altered; Comcast is a troubled stock because its monopoly is collapsing.

That is not only true of its pay-TV monopoly; which was never a complete monopoly and is now in any case so small in margins that Comcast hardly cares what happens to it. It is also true of Comcast’s real power center, the broadband business, which I noted all the way back in 2017 was going to face real trouble as new generations of wireless network technologies came online.

That, not hurricanes, is what almost certainly lies at the core of the disappointing number. Comcast management simply continues to underestimate the impact of widespread deployment of fixed-wireless.

Comcast's Biggest Draw: It's P/E Ratio

The primary argument in favor of keeping Comcast in the portfolio is that it doesn’t need to preserve current profit levels to be a good investment. With the latest decline, Comcast now trades at an almost ridiculous 8.8 TTM P/E ratio. The S&P 500 is 3x that. There are many who argue that Comcast simply can’t fall any further, and it’s time to buy in.

At the outset, it's important to note that the S&P 500 itself has been on a rocket-rollercoaster since the election in November and that 28 P/E ratio itself may be due to come crashing back at some point. But it’s fair to note that even if we compare Comcast’s ratio to a more standard 22 P/E it still looks ludicrously undervalued.

It is also important to keep in mind that Comcast is one of the more heavily leveraged stocks out there. At a debt load of $105 billion, its bondholders account for almost half of its Enterprise Value, roughly 44%. If the broader market reverts to pre-election levels and takes Comcast with it, that ratio could climb over 50%.

Having said all of that, the point is still well-taken; Comcast has ample cushion for shareholders even if earnings take a hit. With bondholders being paid roughly $4.1 billion per year and leaving the other $16 billion to the shareholders, Comcast could afford to see its EBIT fall 30% and still see its stock double, assuming the EV/EBIT multiple reverts to normal.

Spinoff Implications Remain Uncertain

The key question is, can Comcast limit the damage in that way? After years of arguing against the current that Comcast’s cash flow was not as secure as it looked, most of the people I talk to now accept that Comcast will continue to see pressure on its operating margins as fixed-wireless and streaming continue to eat into its broadband and TV operations. But can it hold enough of those pies, combined with continued growth in its mobile operations, to ride out the rough seas and take its stock back up?

Unlike AT&T (T) Comcast does not intend to liquidate its TV operations and take the cash. Rather, it is spinning off the cable networks to its shareholders in a new company. I am not convinced that meaningful value should attach to that asset. Comcast management itself admitted that those networks represent only 2% of Peacock viewing. Some of that could be put down to the fact that most national news is still consumed on linear, not streaming; that statistic may not be the best prism through which to view the value of CNBC and MSNBC.

But it probably does capture the relative values of the sports and scripted elements. As for reality, well, Peacock is in many ways the most reality-friendly of all the streaming services. Any reality content that can’t get traction on Peacock probably doesn’t have much value.

For this article, I am going to assume that the spinoff has a net value of $0. That is almost certainly too harsh; even I think it has some value. But my goal here will be to put a value on the main company; investors can then plug in for themselves whatever spinoff value they think is appropriate to come up with a final number.

NBCUniversal Remains A Bright Spot, But...

Spinoff aside, the media division of Comcast should continue to perform well. But its current margins may still come in for some degree of pressure. The key concern for any media company in the United States right now is that the collapse of the linear bundle is accelerating, and many questions if margins can be preserved in the far more competitive arena of streaming. Comcast does not break out its traditional cable revenue separately from its vMVPD and streaming revenue, nor does it break out broadcast versus cable networks.

However, we do know that this year, Peacock increased revenues by $1.5 billion and EBITDA by $900 million, while total media EBITDA increased only $175 million with the Olympics profits included. Since Olympics profit came to $450 million, EBITDA would have decline $275 million without it, suggesting that last year turning linear revenues into streaming revenues haircut profits by $275 million.

That is a highly imprecise way of estimating, and even I don’t entirely trust it since, as I’ve explained before, the way studios partition revenues and costs between streaming and linear has little rhyme or reason to it, and doesn’t always reflect economic realities. Still, if cord-cutting and attendant Peacock expansion were summing to $275 million a year of declines, four more years of such declines would chop $1.1 billion off of Comcast’s net income.

Then there's the new NBA deal. While many have seen it as a triumphant return for Comcast to the only sport that can rival the NFL on social media, I have noted before that it could produce losses of as much as $1 billion a year over the life of the 11-year contract.

The worst-case scenario for NBCUniversal is if there is a large-scale reversion to mean pricing in the linear TV advertising market. I examined this in depth in a prior article, so I won't be covering all that again. In summary, it is less likely than not, but if it did happen, the hit would probably be in the multi-billion dollar range.

Broadband Broadsides

Comcast’s key business remains domestic broadband, which is generating $26.2 billion in revenue off of a customer base of 31.8 million. At almost $70 per month, Comcast is continuing to charge customers considerably more than wireless competitors Verizon (VZ) and T-Mobile (TMUS) do, with their charges officially at $50 per month but unofficially often available for $35 to their mobile customer base.

The difficulty here for Comcast is that wireline internet is overwhelmingly a fixed-cost business; when Comcast loses a customer its network doesn’t become hardly any less expensive to maintain.

It all comes down to how much Comcast has to cut this number to preserve its broadband business. Were it to cut it all the way to $50, the loss of over 25% of broadband revenue would eat almost $7 billion off of Comcast’s income and make its seemingly bargain-bin price a whole lot less appealing.

Self-Sorting Is Self-Defeating

Comcast management has regularly insisted that it has no intention of doing this because it does not intend to fight T-Mobile and Verizon for every customer. Rather, it intends to let the value-oriented bottom end of the market go to the cheaper services, while it aims to preserve market position at the middle- and higher-end price points.

There is a certain logic to this. Charter (CHTR) reports that cord-cutters are consuming nearly 800 GB per month of data - T-Mobile and Verizon report consumption levels well, well below that, suggesting that indeed it is the data-sippers, at least in relative terms, who are defecting and that heavy data users trust the big pipes of cable more than they desire the low prices of wireless. Comcast management has explicitly stated that their strategy is to wait for Verizon and T-Mobile to run out of room on their networks to steal customers, and then charge their remaining customers similar or even higher prices to what they do now, to preserve margins.

The difficult with that argument is that if consumers are self-sorting in that way, it's actually a negative for Comcast, Altice (ATUS) and other cable companies. Cable companies usually charge the same price to their data-sipping subscribers as they do to their bingers. Officially, there’s a data cap and an overage charge; unofficially, promotional activity centered around “unlimited data included” is very common. That sort of self-sorting means that Verizon and T-Mobile are eating more subscribers and more monthly fees out of Comcast than they would be if FWA subscribers were a more heterogeneous collection of data consumers.

The Future's Winding Road

The real price point going forward for Comcast internet is almost certainly somewhere between its current level and the wireless giants. Management’s determination that they will not cut price doesn’t seem sustainable to me in the long run; going all the way down to the FWA level doesn’t seem necessary.

There are other potential headwinds. Comcast’s protests notwithstanding, last year it finally started offering a substantially cheaper Internet option for value-oriented consumers, the so-called “NOW internet” package. This is priced from $45 to as low as $30 depending on speed and has no data cap. Lower speeds mean it is harder to run over the cap in any event, but it shows that FWA’s margin pressure is very real. I won’t rerun all the math for a broad cut to $30 on the price; obviously, that would be catastrophic, but Comcast has done a reasonably good job so far of segmenting this offer.

Still, you can see now why Comcast’s multiple is so far below the norm. Investors simply don’t know right now what to pencil in for a long-run profit number; the only thing they really know is that management’s assurances that FWA was a minnow that couldn’t take meaningful market share has not borne out. If FWA growth really does taper out quickly and allow Comcast to escape margin pressure, the stock is a screaming bargain; if Comcast ends up having to significantly reprice the base, there could be real problems.

The one thing we haven’t talked about yet is possible upside from mobile, which is now Comcast’s fastest-growing division by consumer count. The issue here is that Comcast continues to refuse to break out this division separately in the numbers. It’s not that I don’t want to talk about it, it’s that I have no numbers to work with. We do know that it generated $3.7 billion in revenue last year, but that number comes without any cost disclosures. We do know it includes devices sales as well as service, and device sales have relatively little margin on them.

Investment Summary

The future of Comcast’s broadband business will be determined by T-Mobile and Verizon’s capacity numbers, not any numbers in Comcast’s earnings reports. It all comes down to just how many customers they are able to take from traditional cable. And what price it will take to stop them. Unease about what that number might be is almost certainly the primary driver of Comcast’s low stock multiple.

I would be surprised if Comcast fell too much further from here, as this company almost certainly has some level of future profit that can safely be relied upon. Even if the wireless pressure is enough to lower future profits to fair value level, it seems unlikely that Comcast is substantially overvalued at these levels.

I set a higher bar for my stocks than the same return I could get from an index fund, however. And I’m not sure if the upside here is as high as the bulls think; I see a larger role for wireless broadband in the future than Comcast management does. And then there is the small, but not meaningless, possibility of crashing through the floor if wireless really outperforms, or if the linear TV ad market does reset after all.

Despite the attractive P/E multiple, I’m going to continue to Avoid for now.

data.title: A Cautionary Tale
data.description:
I always invest with “caution” in mind. Over my 50 years on Wall Street, I have seen wild swings in both directions, and I am mindful of their impact on people’s portfolios. Last Friday, we all watched the equity markets get hit, with the DJIA down -748.63 points. During the last month, according to Bloomberg data, we have witnessed the DJIA -1.69%, the S&P 500 -1.71% and the NASDAQ -2.20%. These are meaningful numbers, especially if you are playing for “appreciation,” which I am currently not doing.

What I have been doing is investing for “income.” As of February 23, 2025, I have nine funds that I am using, that all pay over 20% in dividends. These funds, I point out, are all listed, trade on various exchanges, are public funds and have decent liquidity, in my opinion. Also, all of these funds pay their dividends monthly, meaning you can use the money for living, each month, or use the money to re-invest. In the tax-exempt funds that I use, I have 11 municipal bond funds, all paying monthly, and all have a yield of over 7.00%. Taxable or tax-exempt, I am investing for income.

“If you wish to succeed in life, make perseverance your bosom friend, experience your wise counselor, caution your elder brother, and hope your guardian genius.”

- Joseph Addison

Dividends, as many of you know, are based upon the principal value and not the liquidating value. Consequently, if a fund does not change its dividend, and you re-invested in it last month, your income will be higher from that fund in the new month. This is known as compound interest, and it is a quite valuable tool, in my opinion.

Right now, in my view, the markets are fractured. We have a new President and a host of new pronouncements, executive orders, tariffs and legislation that are causing great uncertainty for the markets, in my view. The bond markets have performed somewhat better, but with the 10-year Treasury yield at 4.43%, we are barely beating our inflation numbers, which do not seem to be dropping anytime soon as the Fed has informed us.

“Never throw caution to the wind. It could whip back into your eyes and blind you.”

- Stephen Colbert

Another note of caution is our pending trade war, which seems to draw nearer each day. The U.S. has imposed 25% tariffs on all imports of steel and aluminum, including supplies from allies, and announced a broader initiative to impose "reciprocal tariffs" across the board. This comes after a discussion about new tariffs for both Canada and Mexico, which I see as a negotiating point, but it is definitely worth some consideration for your investments. While I see President Trump's pronouncements as “forays,” I also recognize the serious implications that could result from them. What is clear is that the old way of doing business has vanished, and President Trump's new policy statements are a significant break from the past. We have entered a new era, and it is one more reason to invest with caution, as our future outcomes are quite uncertain.

“Caution, though very often wasted, is a good risk to take.”

- Josh Billings

Proceed with caution and common sense. That is my mantra for today’s markets.

Original Source: Author

data.title: SoFi Technologies: CEO's Upgraded Guidance Supercharges My Confidence
data.description:
SoFi's (NASDAQ:SOFI) share price has been weak after the release of 4Q24 results on 27 Jan 2025. Share price had fallen almost 18% in two weeks until a recent rebound late last week. While 4Q24 results delivered a double beat in revenue and EPS, I believe the sharp decline in share price reflects investors' disappointment in management's 2025 EPS guidance and skepticism about the validity of SoFi's long-term EPS guidance for 2026 where management communicated back in 4Q23 and reiterated in the 4Q24 results conference call.

I was also disappointed by the 2025 guidance and worried about whether the 2026 guidance is still relevant, but CEO Anthony Noto's latest Q&A during BofA 2025 Financial Services Conference seems subtly raising the guidance for 2026. His 20-30% ROE target and over 50% revenue contribution from fee-based businesses reinforce my confidence in SoFi of being a financial technology company rather than a traditional bank.

I rate Strong Buy on SoFi. My base case price target is set at $19 based on 25x P/E and an EPS estimate of $0.76/sh in 2026. If things turn out to be stronger than my estimate, share price could head towards my blue-sky price target of $23 based on 30x FY26 P/E. I believe SoFi has huge growth potential and the recent share price weakness offers an excellent buying opportunity.

Let's take a look at SoFi's 4Q24 results & 2025 Guidance

CEO Anthony Noto stated in 4Q24 results press release that "2025 was SoFi's best year ever". He is indeed correct if you look at SoFi's double beat 4Q24 results. Adjusted revenue grew 24% yoy to $739m, beating consensus estimate of 11% growth. Adjusted EPS reached $0.05 taking full year adjusted EPS to $0.15 which was 30% ahead of management guidance of $0.11-0.12 during 3Q24 results. Therefore, it is hard to believe that a rational investor would dump SoFi' shares on the back of this amazing results.

So, what triggered the share price collapse on 27 Jan? The likely culprit is SoFi's conservative EPS guidance for 2025. Besides guiding revenue to grow 23-26%, management expect EPS to be $0.25-0.27 in 2025, representing 73% yoy growth based on the mid-point guided EPS range. This might not seem concerning at first glance. However, recall that management had projected an EPS of $0.55-0.80 in 2026 during 4Q23 earnings call and this is up to 3x higher than the 2025 guidance. This big contrast likely fueled investor skepticism and contributed to the sharp decline in share price after results.

Is the concern overdone? Management has a Strong Track Record in Beating its Guidance

SoFi's management is good at managing investors' expectation. In 2024, management has raised its earnings guidance twice. Compared the actual adjusted diluted EPS to the original guidance, actual EPS was double.

Looking at Earnings Surprise data collected by Seeking Alpha, there was only one earnings miss out of the last 12 quarters. This suggests that management has a strong tendency to give conservative guidance. Therefore, it is not unreasonable to assume the $0.27 adjusted diluted EPS for 2025 could have been under-guided.

Anthony Noto has subtly raised guidance for 2026 during BofA conference

While the management team reiterating the old guidance for 2026 during the 4Q23 earnings call, CEO Anthony Noto gave me a big surprise when he talked about the growth outlook for SoFi during BofA 2025 Financial Services Conference on 11 Feb 2025. On revenue, instead of 20-25% CAGR growth, he said, "We believe the opportunities for growth are so significant that we just raised our 2023 to 2026 revenue CAGR to more than 25%."

More importantly, he said, "We have more confidence than ever in our 2026 EPS guidance of $0.55 to $0.80 per share". Compared to investors' skepticism, this is such a big contrast.

In addition, he said, "we believe we can drive 20% to 30% return on equity as the business continues to scale and matures". On margins, he said, "we believe our EBITDA margins long term are 30%, and our GAAP net income margins long term are 20%."

By using the new guidance, I estimate SoFi's EPS in 2026 to be $0.76. This is close to the high bar of the guidance range of $0.8.

SoFi is Not a Traditional Bank

Technology Platform + Financial Service will soon take up half of the business

SoFi has three business segments, namely Lending, Technology Platform and Financial Service. Compared their growth in 2024, Technology Platform + Financial Service net revenue growth was superior at 54% vs 8% in traditional lending business. More importantly, Technology Platform + Financial Service revenue already accounted for 47%, a big jump from 38% in 2023.

Fee-based revenue will eventually take up >50% of total revenue

Another thing making SoFi less like a traditional bank is the surging fee-based revenue. SoFi made a total of $970m fee-based revenue in 2024 which grew at skyrocket speed of 75%. This also represented 34% of total revenue. Compared to lending business, fee-based business requires less capital and hence less exposure to credit risk but generating higher ROE. CEO Anthony Noto said, "You can expect that our fee-based businesses and revenue that continues to increase well over 50% of our total revenue". I have never seen a traditional bank having over 50% of its revenue coming from fee-based business. This could be another evidence suggesting SoFi is not a traditional bank.

20-30% ROE is not a traditional bank that can achieve

SoFi's ROE was 8.5% in 2024. However, CEO Anthony Noto aims to bring SoFi's ROE to 20-30% in the long term by leveraging technology and fee-based revenue model. With my earlier net income estimate, SoFi's ROE could reach 13% in 2026. The below chart shows that Wall Street banks had ROE ranging from 6 to 17% in 2024. If we look back since 2015, the highest recorded ROE was 22% by Goldman Sachs in 3Q21 and higher than 20% ROE only happened once in the past decade. This suggests that a 20-30% ROE is well above the norm for traditional banks.

Valuation: SoFi should be valued as a high growth Fintech

While SoFi is a bank holding company with a charter, its business is becoming less like a traditional bank. If SoFi could sustain a 20-30% ROE in the long term, there is no reason to benchmark SoFi to the lower ROE Wall Street banks. In reality, it is more reasonable to benchmark SoFi to high growth Fintech companies, where they could trade up to 40x P/E.

For conservative purpose, I believe 20-30x P/E could be a target multiple range for SoFi. Based on my EPS estimate of $0.76 in 2026 and 25x P/E, I arrive at a price target of $19. If under the blue-sky scenario, my target price could be raised to $23 based on 30x P/E.

Downside Risks to my Buy Recommendation

If SoFi's earnings don't exceed management guidance in 2025, it might trigger another round of sell off as this casts doubts on the long-term $0.55-0.8 EPS guidance in 2026.

A ROE of 20-30% is a key matrix differentiating SoFi from other traditional banks. Hence, its ROE improvement would be closely monitored by investors going forward. Any signs of ROE improvement slow down or even decline could lead to negative sentiment on the stock.

Conclusion

My buy recommendation on SoFi is based on my belief that the market has mistakenly sold off the stock without much considering management's track record in guidance and investors' expectation management. I believe the latest EPS guidance of $0.25-0.27 for 2025 is way too conservative vs my estimate of $0.4. If SoFi continues to beat consensus in 1Q25 and 2Q25, I believe the market should regain confidence in its 2026 EPS guidance of $0.55-0.8. In addition, CEO Anthony Noto's long-term ROE target of 20-30% could also be a key re-rating factor as the market sees improving ROE and start to believe SoFi is not a traditional bank but more of a high growth financial technology company.
